code:
inputs = tf.keras.Input(shape=(64, 313, 1))
x = backbone_model(inputs, training=False)

# Baseline head: Feature refinement via BN/Dropout
x = tf.keras.layers.Dropout(0.3)(x)
x = tf.keras.layers.BatchNormalization()(x)
x = tf.keras.layers.Dense(512, activation='relu')(x)
x = tf.keras.layers.Dropout(0.3)(x)

# Output layer for 234 multi-label species classification
outputs = tf.keras.layers.Dense(234, activation='sigmoid')(x)
model = tf.keras.Model(inputs, outputs)

llm_analysis: This result establishes a solid baseline, achieving a commendable $\text{val\_auc}$ of 0.8847 with low validation loss, indicating the model has successfully learned discriminative features. The existing architecture is robust, but to improve performance, the primary suspect is the final classification layer's activation. If the 234 bird species are mutually exclusive (a single sample belongs to only one class), the final output layer should switch from $\text{sigmoid}$ to $\text{softmax}$ and the loss function should change to $\text{categorical\_crossentropy}$ to enforce proper probability distributions, which will likely boost $\text{val\_auc}$.
code:
inputs = tf.keras.Input(shape=(64, 313, 1))
x = backbone_model(inputs, training=False) # x has shape (None, 1280)

# Baseline head design: Using sequential Dense layers for feature refinement.

# 1. Initial Dropout for regularization
x = Dropout(0.3)(x)

# 2. First dense layer to expand/refine feature space
x = Dense(1024, activation='relu')(x)

# 3. Batch Normalization for stability
x = BatchNormalization()(x)

# 4. Second dense layer to focus on discrimination
x = Dense(512, activation='relu')(x)

# 5. Final classification layer
outputs = Dense(234, activation='sigmoid')(x)

model = Model(inputs, outputs)

# This sequence of Dense layers, Dropout regularization, and non
llm_analysis: The current result shows strong performance, with the `val_auc` of 0.9044 exceeding the previous best run, indicating the model is generalizing well across the diverse species labels. The structure itself is sound, employing a robust backbone and necessary regularization (Dropout) for multi-label classification. To further improve the `val_auc`, I recommend systematically tuning the Dropout rates or experimenting with reducing the size of the intermediate bottleneck layers (e.g., from 1024 to 768) to force the model to learn a more concise and discriminative feature set.
code:
inputs = tf.keras.Input(shape=(64, 313, 1))
x = backbone_model(inputs, training=False)

# Feature refinement block (MLP bottleneck structure)
x = Dense(1024, activation='relu')(x)
x = Dropout(0.4)(x)
x = Dense(512, activation='relu')(x)
x = Dropout(0.3)(x)

# Final multi-label classification head
outputs = Dense(234, activation='sigmoid')(x)
model = Model(inputs, outputs)
